Ayahs

حمٓ (١)
Ha, Meem. (1)
تَنزِيلُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ (٢)
The [gradual] sending down of the Book is from Allah, the All-mighty, All-wise. (2)
إِنَّ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٣)
In the heavens and the earth there are indeed signs for the faithful, (3)
وَفِى خَلْقِكُمْ وَمَا يَبُثُّ مِن دَآبَّةٍ ءَايَـٰتٌ لِّقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ (٤)
There are signs for people who have certainty in your own creation and in animals, He scatters abroad. (4)
وَٱخْتِلَـٰفِ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ وَمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مِن رِّزْقٍ فَأَحْيَا بِهِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَا وَتَصْرِيفِ ٱلرِّيَـٰحِ ءَايَـٰتٌ لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ (٥)
There are signs for people who exercise their reason in the alternation of night and day, in the provision that Allah sends down from the sky, with which He revives the earth after its death, and in the changing of the winds. (5)
تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱللَّهِ نَتْلُوهَا عَلَيْكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ فَبِأَىِّ حَدِيثٍۭ بَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ وَءَايَـٰتِهِۦ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٦)
These are the signs of Allah that We recite for you in truth. So what discourse will they believe after Allah and His signs? (6)

Contents of Surah al-Jathiyah

This Surah, which is the sixth of the "Hawamim" Surahs, is a Meccan Surah. It was revealed at a time when a fierce conflict between the "Muslims" and the "polytheists" dominated the social atmosphere of Mecca. For this reason, it focuses primarily on issues related to Monotheism (Tawhid), the struggle against polytheism, threatening the oppressors with the Court of Resurrection, drawing attention to the recording of deeds, and reflecting on the fate of defiant past nations. The content of this Surah can be summarized into seven sections:

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 21; p. 223

1- The greatness of the Holy Quran and its importance.

2- Presenting some proofs of Monotheism against the polytheists.

3- Mentioning some claims of the materialists and providing a decisive response to them.

4- A brief reference to the fate of some past nations, such as the Children of Israel, as a testimony to the discussions of this Surah.

5- A severe warning to the misguided who persist and insist on their deviant beliefs.

6- An invitation to forgiveness and pardon alongside decisiveness and not deviating from the path of Truth.

7- Vivid references to the soul-shaking events of the Resurrection, especially the Record of Deeds which encompasses all of man's actions without omission.

This Surah begins with the great attributes and names of Allah, such as "the Almighty" (al-Aziz) and "the All-Wise" (al-Hakim), and concludes with them as well. The name of this Surah is "al-Jathiyah" in accordance with verse 28 of this Surah (Jathiyah means "the one kneeling"), referring to the state of many people on the scene of the Resurrection in the Divine Court of Justice.

The late Tabarsi in "Majma' al-Bayan" has cited another name for this Surah, which is not very well-known, and that is "al-Shari'ah," in accordance with verse 18 of this Surah.

The Virtue of Reciting Surah al-Jathiyah

In a Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

"Whoever recites Ha-Mim al-Jathiyah, Allah will conceal his faults and calm his fear at the time of Reckoning." [^1]

In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"Whoever recites Surah al-Jathiyah (with thought and reflection that serves as a prelude to action), his reward is that he shall never see the Fire of Hell, nor hear the sighing or groaning of Hell, and he shall be a companion of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله." [^2]

[^1]: Tafsir "Majma' al-Bayan," beginning of Surah al-Jathiyah.

[^2]: Tafsir "al-Burhan," beginning of Surah al-Jathiyah, Vol. 4, p. 167.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

حم (1) تَنْزِيلُ الْكِتابِ مِنَ اللَّهِ الْعَزِيزِ الْحَكِيمِ (2) إِنَّ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ لَآياتٍ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (3) وَ فِي خَلْقِكُمْ وَ ما يَبُثُّ مِنْ دابَّةٍ آياتٌ لِقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ (4)

وَ اخْتِلافِ اللَّيْلِ وَ النَّهارِ وَ ما أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ مِنَ السَّماءِ مِنْ رِزْقٍ فَأَحْيا بِهِ الْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِها وَ تَصْرِيفِ الرِّياحِ آياتٌ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ (5) تِلْكَ آياتُ اللَّهِ نَتْلُوها عَلَيْكَ بِالْحَقِّ فَبِأَيِّ حَدِيثٍ بَعْدَ اللَّهِ وَ آياتِهِ يُوقِنُونَ (6)

Commentary

His Signs Are Everywhere

We have said that this Surah is the sixth Surah beginning with the disjointed letters (Muqatta'at) "Ha-Mim," and together with the following Surah, namely Surah al-Ahqaf, they constitute the seven "Hawamim" Surahs.

Regarding the interpretation of the "disjointed letters," we have repeatedly discussed them at the beginning of Surahs al-Baqarah, Al-Imran, and al-A'raf, as well as the Ha-Mim Surahs.

The famous commentator Tabarsi adds at the beginning of this Surah: "The best statement is to say that 'Ha-Mim' is the name of this Surah." Then he quotes from some commentators: "Naming this Surah 'Ha-Mim' is to indicate that this Quran, which is entirely a miracle, is composed of the letters of the alphabet."

Yes, this Book, which is light, guidance, a leader, a path-opener, and the eternal miracle of the Prophet of Islam, has been brought into existence from the combination of these very simple letters. This is the ultimate greatness—that such a momentous matter is formed from such simple means.

Perhaps for this reason, He immediately mentions the greatness of the Quran, saying: "The revelation of the Book is from Allah, the Almighty, the All-Wise" (تَنْزِيلُ الْكِتابِ مِنَ اللَّهِ الْعَزِيزِ الْحَكِيمِ) [^1]. "Al-Aziz" means the Powerful and Invincible, and "al-Hakim" means the One who is aware of the secrets of all things and whose every action is based on calculation and wisdom. It is clear that to reveal such a Book, infinite wisdom and unlimited power are required, which exist in none but Allah Almighty.

Interestingly, this verse appears in this exact form at the beginning of four Surahs of the Quran: three from the Hawamim (Mu'min, Jathiyah, and Ahqaf) and one from the non-Hawamim (Surah al-Zumar). This repetition and emphasis are intended to draw everyone's attention to the depth of the Quran's secrets and the greatness of its content, so that they do not regard any expression in it as simple, nor think any word is without calculation, and do not settle for any limited level of understanding and perception of it.

It is also noteworthy that sometimes the description "Aziz" (Mighty/Invincible) is mentioned for the Quran itself, such as: "And indeed, it is a Mighty Book" (وَ إِنَّهُ لَكِتابٌ عَزِيزٌ) (Fussilat: 41). The hands of vain-talkers do not reach its hem, the passage of time does not diminish its value, and its truths do not grow old; it exposes the distorters and always advances through the course of time.

And sometimes it is used for the One who revealed the Quran, as in the verse under discussion, and both are correct.

Then, He proceeds to explain the signs and tokens of Allah's greatness in the horizons and within the souls, saying: "Indeed, in the heavens and the earth are signs for those who believe and seek the truth" (ان فى السماوات و الارض لآيات للمؤمنين). The greatness of the heavens on one hand, and their amazing system—which has continued for millions of years with its programs proceeding without the slightest deviation or change—on the other hand, and the structure of the earth and its wonders on a third hand, each is a sign among the signs of Allah.

The earth, which according to some scientists has 14 types of motion, rotates around itself with dizzying speed, and also has a rapid motion around the sun, and another motion along with the solar system family in the heart of the galaxy, engaged in an endless journey. Yet, it is so calm that it is a cradle of comfort for man and all living creatures, and it is not felt at all that it moves even by a needle's tip! It is not so hard that one cannot farm or build houses on it, nor so soft that it cannot be settled upon or endure.

All kinds of resources, minerals, and means of life have been provided in it for billions of humans of the past, present, and future, and it possesses such beauty that it enchants and fascinates man. The mountains, the seas, and the atmosphere are each a mysterious sign. However, He places these signs of Monotheism and the Creator's greatness only at the disposal of the believers—that is, the seekers of truth and the wayfarers of the path of "Allah"—while the ignorant and arrogant blind-hearted are deprived of perceiving them.

Then, from these "external" signs, He turns to the "internal" signs, saying: "And in your creation and the living creatures He has scattered [across the earth] are signs for a people who are certain" (وَ فِي خَلْقِكُمْ وَ ما يَبُثُّ مِنْ دابَّةٍ آياتٌ لِقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ).

In truth, as stated in the famous expression attributed to the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام: "This human is a small body in which the great universe is summarized," and whatever exists in the great universe, a sample of it exists within his body and soul.

His traits and attributes are a mixture of the traits and attributes of the collection of living creatures and beings, and the diversity of his creation is an essence of the collection of events of this great world.

The structure of a single cell of his is as mysterious as the structure of a vast industrial city, and the creation of a single hair of his, with the various features and characteristics discovered by the hand of science and knowledge, is itself a great sign among the Divine signs.

The existence of thousands of kilometers of large and small vessels and extremely delicate capillaries in his body, and thousands of kilometers of communication fibers and telegraph wires of the nervous system, and how they connect to the extremely complex and mysterious yet powerful command center in the brain, and the way each of the internal organs of the body works, and their amazing coordination during sudden events, and the fierce defense of the body's protective forces against the invasion of external factors—each of these alone is a sign.

And beyond man, there are hundreds of thousands of types of living creatures, from microscopic animals to giant beasts, with completely diverse and colorful features and structures, such that sometimes a group of scientists must spend their entire lives studying just one type. Despite thousands of books being written about the secrets of their creation, what we know about them is still very little compared to what we do not know. Yes, each in its turn is a sign and a token of the infinite knowledge, wisdom, and power of the Source of creation.

But why do a group of people spend decades moving amidst these signs and yet have not the slightest awareness of even one of them? The reason is what the Quran says:

"These signs are specifically for the seekers of faith and certainty and the possessors of thought and reflection"—for those who have opened the doors of their hearts and are thirsty with their whole being for certainty, science, and knowledge. They do not overlook even the slightest movement or the smallest creature; they reflect upon it for hours and make it a ladder to ascend toward "Allah" and a ledger for "knowing the Creator," and they engage in intimate prayer with Him, filling the cup of their hearts with the wine of His love.

In the next verse, He mentions "three great blessings," each of which plays an important role in the life of man and other living creatures, and each is a sign among the signs of Allah: the issue of "light," "water," and "air." He says: "And in the alternation of night and day, and the provision that Allah has sent down from the sky and thereby given life to the earth after its death, and in the shifting of the winds, are signs for a people who reason" (وَ اخْتِلافِ اللَّيْلِ وَ النَّهارِ وَ ما أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ مِنَ السَّماءِ مِنْ رِزْقٍ فَأَحْيا بِهِ الْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِها وَ تَصْرِيفِ الرِّياحِ آياتٌ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ). The issue of the system of "light and darkness" and the alternation of night and day, each succeeding and replacing the other with a specific order, is highly calculated and amazing. Had the day been permanent or excessively long, the heat would have risen so much that all living beings would have burned; and had the night been eternal or very long, all would have frozen from the intensity of the cold!

There is also the possibility in the interpretation of the verse that "alternation" (ikhtilaf) does not mean succeeding one another, but rather refers to the difference that night and day acquire in the seasons of the year, as a result of which various products, plants, fruits, and the descent of snow, rain, and other blessings are granted to humans.

Interestingly, scientists say that despite all the differences that various regions on earth have regarding the length of night and day, if we calculate the total days of the year, all regions benefit from sunlight exactly the same amount! [^1] In the second stage, the life-giving heavenly provision, namely rain, is mentioned, about whose refined nature there is no need for words, nor about its life-giving power; everywhere it is a sign of life, freshness, and beauty.

Why should it not be so, when the main part of the human body and many other animals and plants is composed of this very water?

And in the third case, the talk is of the shifting of the winds—winds that move the life-giving oxygenated air and place it at the disposal of living beings, sending carbon-polluted air to the plains, forests, and deserts for purification, and after purification, carrying it back to cities and settlements. It is wonderful that these two groups of living beings, namely "animals" and "plants," act exactly opposite to each other: the former takes oxygen and gives carbon dioxide, and the latter takes carbon and gives oxygen, so that balance is maintained in the system of life, and the earth's reserve of useful air is not destroyed over time.

The blowing of winds which, in addition to this, are the pollinators of plants, the fertilizers of them, the scatterers of various seeds in different lands, and the nurturers of natural pastures and forests, and the creators of waves in the heart of the oceans—waves that give life and movement to the sea and protect the water from stench and corruption. Also, these same winds move ships across the surface of the oceans. [^1]

Interestingly, in the above verses, He first speaks of the signs of the heavens and the earth and says at the end: in these are signs for the "believers." Then He mentions the creation of living beings and says: in these are signs for the "people of certainty." After that, He discusses the system of light and darkness, wind and rain, and says: in these are signs for the "people of reason."

This difference in expression might be because man traverses three stages in the path of knowing Allah to reach the destination: first, the stage of "reflection" (tafakkur); then the stage of "certainty" (yaqin) and knowledge; and after that, the stage of "faith" (iman) and the so-called binding of the heart. Since in terms of nobility, faith is the first stage, certainty the second, and reflection the third, this order is mentioned in the verses, even though in terms of external existence, reflection is in the first stage, then certainty, and then faith. In other words, those who are people of faith ascend to this high stage from observing Divine signs, and those who are not should at least reach the stage of certainty, or at the very least, the stage of reflection.

Commentators have mentioned other aspects in this regard, but what we have said is more appropriate.

Then, in the last verse under discussion, as a summary of the previous discussions and an expression of the greatness and importance of the Quranic verses, He says: "These are the signs of Allah which We recite to you in truth" (تِلْكَ آياتُ اللَّهِ نَتْلُوها عَلَيْكَ بِالْحَقِ).

[^1]: Regarding the "alternation of night and day," detailed discussions have appeared in Surah al-Baqarah in Volume 1 under verse 164, in Volume 3 of Al-Imran under verse 190, in Volume 8 under verse 6 of Yunus, and in Volume 16 under verse 71 of Surah al-Qasas.

[^1]: Regarding the effects of wind and rain, detailed discussions have appeared in Volume 16 under verses 46 to 50 of Surah al-Rum (page 463).

Does the word "tilka" (these) refer to the Quranic verses? Or the signs and tokens of Allah in the horizons and souls mentioned in the previous verses? Both possibilities have been suggested.

However, apparently, by the evidence of the expression "recite" (natluha), the meaning is the Quranic verses. Yet, these very Quranic verses are the explainers of Allah's signs throughout the entire universe, and thus both interpretations can be reconciled (reflect on this).

In any case, "tilawah" (recitation) is from the root "tuluw" (on the weight of fikr), meaning to bring one word after another. Therefore, the recitation of Quranic verses is the reading of verses one after another.

The expression "in truth" (bil-haqq) refers both to the content of these verses and to the truthfulness of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله prophethood and Divine revelation. In other words: these verses are so eloquent and reasoned that the proof of their truthfulness and the truthfulness of the one who brought them is inherent within them.

Truly, if they do not believe in these signs, in what will they believe? Therefore, at the end of the verse, He adds: "Then in what discourse after Allah and His signs will they believe?" (فَبِأَيِّ حَدِيثٍ بَعْدَ اللَّهِ وَ آياتِهِ يُؤْمِنُونَ) [^1] According to Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, "hadith" (discourse/narrative) refers to the cautionary stories of past nations and their instructive biographies, while "ayat" (signs) refers to the proofs that distinguish truth from falsehood, and the verses of the Holy Quran speak of both.

Yes, truly the Holy Quran possesses such content in terms of Monotheistic reasoning and proofs, as well as in terms of advice and exhortation, that it invites any heart with the slightest readiness and any head with a passion for truth toward Allah, purity, and piety. If these clear signs do not affect someone, there is never any hope for his guidance.

[^1]: The expression "after Allah" (ba'da Allah) has an omitted part, which is estimated as: "Then in what discourse after the discourse of Allah (hadith Allah)..."

Ayahs

وَيْلٌ لِّكُلِّ أَفَّاكٍ أَثِيمٍ (٧)
Woe to every sinful liar, (7)
يَسْمَعُ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِ ثُمَّ يُصِرُّ مُسْتَكْبِرًا كَأَن لَّمْ يَسْمَعْهَا ۖ فَبَشِّرْهُ بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ (٨)
who hears the signs of Allah being recited to him, yet persists arrogantly as if he had not heard them. So inform him of a painful punishment. (8)
وَإِذَا عَلِمَ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا شَيْـًٔا ٱتَّخَذَهَا هُزُوًا ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ (٩)
Should he learn anything about Our signs, he takes them in derision. For such there is a humiliating punishment. (9)
مِّن وَرَآئِهِمْ جَهَنَّمُ ۖ وَلَا يُغْنِى عَنْهُم مَّا كَسَبُوا۟ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا مَا ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ أَوْلِيَآءَ ۖ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (١٠)
Ahead of them is hell, and neither what they have earned, nor what they had taken as guardians besides Allah will avail them in any way, and there is a great punishment for them. (10)

Commentary

Woe to the Sinful Liar!

The preceding verses indicated that there is a group who hear the Divine words—supported by various monotheistic proofs, exhortations, and counsels—yet these words leave no impact upon them.

The verses under discussion speak in detail about this group and the consequences of their actions.

First, He says: "Woe to every sinful liar!" (وَيْلٌ لِكُلِّ أَفَّاكٍ أَثِيمٍ). The word "Affak" is an intensive form (mubalaghah), referring to someone who lies excessively; it is also sometimes applied to one who tells a "great lie," even if not frequently.

"Athim" is derived from the root "ithm," meaning a criminal or sinner, and it also conveys an intensive meaning.

From this verse, it becomes clearly evident that taking a hostile stance against Divine verses is the work of those who are entirely polluted by sin and falsehood, not those of pure nature and truthfulness.

Then, referring to the nature of their stance, He adds: "He constantly hears the verses of Allah being recited to him, yet he persists in opposition out of arrogance, as if he had not heard them at all" (يَسْمَعُ آياتِ اللَّهِ تُتْلى‌ عَلَيْهِ ثُمَّ يُصِرُّ مُسْتَكْبِراً كَأَنْ لَمْ يَسْمَعْها). [^1]

[^1]: "يَسْمَعُ آياتِ اللَّهِ" may be an "initial sentence" (jumla musta'nafah) or another "description" (wasf) for "كل" (every).

In this manner, being tainted by sin and lies, as well as arrogance and self-conceit, causes him to ignore all these verses and feign deafness, just as stated in Surah Luqman, verse 7: "وَ إِذا تُتْلى‌ عَلَيْهِ آياتُنا وَلَّى مُسْتَكْبِراً كَأَنْ لَمْ يَسْمَعْها كَأَنَّ فِي أُذُنَيْهِ وَقْراً" (And when Our verses are recited to him, he turns away arrogantly as if he had not heard them, as if there were deafness in his ears).

At the end of the verse, He severely threatens them with a harsh punishment, saying: "Give such a person the glad tidings of a painful punishment!" (فَبَشِّرْهُ بِعَذابٍ أَلِيمٍ).

Just as he caused pain to the heart of the Prophet and the believers, We shall afflict him with a painful punishment, for the punishment of the Resurrection is the embodiment of human deeds today! Although some commentators have mentioned a specific occasion of revelation for this and the following verse, attributing it to "Abu Jahl" or "Nadr bin al-Harith"—who collected Persian stories and legends to distract people and turn them away from the true religion—it is obvious that it is not restricted to them. Rather, it is not even exclusive to the Arab polytheists; it encompasses all arrogant, lying criminals in every age and time—those who ignore Divine verses, the messages of the Prophets, and the words of great leaders because they do not align with their deviant desires and passions, do not confirm their satanic thoughts, and do not agree with their wrong habits and blind imitations. Yes, give all of them the glad tidings of a painful punishment.

Although "punishment" is not typically associated with "glad tidings" (basharat), this expression is used as a form of mockery and humiliation for this group.

Then He adds: "And whenever this stubborn, arrogant person learns anything of Our verses, he takes them in ridicule" (وَ إِذا عَلِمَ مِنْ آياتِنا شَيْئاً اتَّخَذَها هُزُواً). [^1] In reality, these self-centered ignorant ones possess two states: the first state is that they often hear the Divine verses and ignore them, passing by with indifference as if they never heard them. The second state is that if they do hear and intend to pay attention or show a reaction, they do nothing but mock and ridicule. All of them share these two programs—sometimes this, sometimes that (therefore, there is no contradiction between this verse and the previous one).

It is interesting that He first says: "If he learns anything of Our verses." But He does not say afterward that he mocks what he has learned; rather, He says he takes all Our verses (both those he learned and those he did not) in mockery and ridicule!

This is the height of ignorance and unawareness—that a person denies or mocks something they have not understood at all. This is the best evidence of their stubbornness and enmity.

At the end of the verse, He describes the punishment of this group as follows: "For them is a humiliating punishment" (أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ مُهِينٌ).

Why should it not be so? While they sought to gain status and personality for themselves by mocking Divine verses, Allah, as a penalty for this act, makes them lowly, disgraced, and insignificant, trapping them in a humiliating and degrading punishment on the Day of Resurrection. They will be dragged on their faces and taken to Hell in chains and shackles, accompanied by the reproach and blame of the angels of punishment.

[^1]: Note that the pronoun in "اتَّخَذَها" does not refer back to "شَيْئاً" (anything) but rather to "آياتِنا" (Our verses).

From here, it becomes clear why in the previous verse the punishment was described as "painful" (alim), here as "humiliating" (muhin), and in the next verse as "great" (azim). In truth, each is proportionate to the nature of their sin!

The next verse describes this "humiliating punishment" as follows: "Behind them is Hell" (مِنْ وَرائِهِمْ جَهَنَّمُ). The expression "behind them," even though Hell is in front of them and they will reach it in the future, may be from the perspective that they have turned toward the world and ignored the Hereafter and Divine punishment, casting it behind their backs. It is common to say of anything a person ignores: "He has put it behind him." The Holy Quran says: "إِنَّ هؤُلاءِ يُحِبُّونَ الْعاجِلَةَ وَ يَذَرُونَ‌ ورائهم يوما ثقيلا" (Indeed, these people love the fleeting life and leave behind them a heavy Day) (Al-Insan: 27). A group of commentators has also said that the word "wara" is derived from the root "muwarat," referring to anything hidden from a person; it is applied both to what is "behind" and what is "in front" when it is distant and hidden. Thus, the word "wara" has a comprehensive concept applied to two opposite instances. [^1] It also does not seem far-fetched to say that the expression "wara" refers to the issue of cause and effect. For example, we say: "If you eat that bad food, illness is behind it," meaning eating the food is the cause of that illness. Here too, their deeds are the factor and cause of the humiliating punishment of Hell.

[^1]: Some have also said that "wara" means "behind" if added to the subject (fa'il), and "in front" if added to the object (maf'ul) (Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 8, p. 439), but there is no evidence for this claim.

In any case, the verse continues: If they imagine that their abundant wealth and their idols and fabricated gods will solve any of their problems, they are gravely mistaken, for "never will what they earned avail them anything, nor those they took as protectors besides Allah" (وَ لا يُغْنِي عَنْهُمْ ما كَسَبُوا شَيْئاً وَ لا مَا اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ أَوْلِياءَ).

And since there is no way of escape or salvation, they must remain in the fire of Divine wrath and anger: "And for them is a great punishment" (وَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ).

They belittled the Divine verses, so Allah makes their punishment great; they acted with pride, so Allah gives them a great punishment!

This punishment is great from every aspect: it is eternal, it is severe, it is coupled with humiliation, and it penetrates to the depths of the bones and souls of the sinners. Yes, a great sin against the Great Lord warrants a great punishment as its penalty.

Ayahs

هَـٰذَا هُدًى ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مِّن رِّجْزٍ أَلِيمٌ (١١)
This is [true] guidance, and there is a painful punishment due to defilement for those who defy the signs of their Lord. (11)
۞ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى سَخَّرَ لَكُمُ ٱلْبَحْرَ لِتَجْرِىَ ٱلْفُلْكُ فِيهِ بِأَمْرِهِۦ وَلِتَبْتَغُوا۟ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (١٢)
It is Allah who disposed the sea for you[r benefit] so that the ships may sail in it by His command, that you may seek of His bounty and that you may give thanks. (12)
وَسَخَّرَ لَكُم مَّا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا مِّنْهُ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ (١٣)
He has disposed for you[r benefit] whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth; all is from Him. There are indeed signs in that for a people who reflect. (13)
قُل لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ يَغْفِرُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ لَا يَرْجُونَ أَيَّامَ ٱللَّهِ لِيَجْزِىَ قَوْمًۢا بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ (١٤)
Say to the faithful to forgive those who do not expect Allah’s days, that He may [Himself] requite every people for what they used to earn. (14)
مَنْ عَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَلِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَنْ أَسَآءَ فَعَلَيْهَا ۖ ثُمَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكُمْ تُرْجَعُونَ (١٥)
Whoever acts righteously, it is for his own soul, and whoever does evil, it is to its own detriment, then you will be brought back to your Lord. (15)

Commentary

All are Bewildered and Subservient to You!

Following the discussions regarding the greatness of Divine signs in the previous verses, the verses under discussion pursue the same meaning, stating: "This [Quran] is a guidance" (هذا هدى).

It distinguishes truth from falsehood, illuminates the scene of human life, and takes the hand of those traveling the path of truth to lead them to the intended destination.

"But for those who deny the signs of their Lord, there is a painful punishment of foul wrath" (وَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِآياتِ رَبِّهِمْ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ مِنْ رِجْزٍ أَلِيمٌ‌).

The word "Rijz" (rhyming with hirs), as Raghib has stated in Al-Mufradat, originally means agitation, trembling, and disorder. Specifically, when a camel becomes ill such that it takes short, irregular steps due to extreme weakness, the Arabs call this state "Rijz."

This word is also applied to the plague, severe calamities, intense snow or hail, the whisperings of Satans, and the like, because all of them cause agitation, instability, and disorder. If war poetry is called "Rajaz" (rhyming with gharadh), it is because of its short, closely-spaced segments (or because of the instability and agitation it casts upon the enemy's body).

Then, the discourse shifts to the topic of Monotheism (Tawhid) which was raised in the opening verses of this Surah, providing effective lessons in the recognition of God to the polytheists. Sometimes He appeals to their emotions, saying: "It is Allah who has subjected the sea for you so that ships may sail upon it by His command, and that you may seek of His bounty, and perhaps you will be grateful" (اللَّهُ الَّذِي سَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الْبَحْرَ لِتَجْرِيَ الْفُلْكُ فِيهِ بِأَمْرِهِ وَ لِتَبْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِهِ وَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ‌).

Who created this property in the raw material of ships so that they do not sink in water?

And who made the water a soft bed for their movement so they can easily advance through it? And who commanded the power of the wind to blow regularly across the surface of the oceans and set the ships in motion? (Or substituted the power of steam for the wind to drive these massive vessels at great speed).

We know that the greatest and most important means of transport for humanity, both in the past and today, has been ships—small, large, and giant—which throughout the year carry millions of people and even more commercial goods from the farthest points of the globe to various regions. Sometimes a ship is as large as a small city in terms of size and inhabitants, containing vast equipment and wealth!

Truly, if these three forces did not exist, how could man solve his transportation problems with simple, ordinary mounts? Although simple mounts are also among His blessings and are effective in their own place.

Interestingly, in verse 32 of Surah Ibrahim, He says: وَ سَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الْفُلْكَ لِتَجْرِيَ فِي الْبَحْرِ بِأَمْرِهِ‌: "He subjected the ships to you so that they sail in the sea by His command."

But here He says: "He subjected the sea to you so that ships may sail in it," because there the focus is more on the subjection of the seas; hence, it is followed by "and He subjected the rivers to you" (وَ سَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الْأَنْهارَ). However, here the focus is on the subjection of the ships. In any case, both are subjected to man by Allah's command and are at his service.

The purpose of this subjection is that you may "seek of Divine bounty," an expression usually used regarding trade and economic activities, though it also encompasses the transport and movement of passengers.

The goal of this utilization of Divine bounty is to stimulate the human sense of gratitude, so that their emotions are mobilized to thank the Benefactor, and subsequently, they may set foot on the path of knowing Allah (Ma'rifatullah).

The word "Fulk" (ship), as we have mentioned before, is used for both singular and plural.

For more details regarding the subjection of the seas, ships, and their benefits and blessings, please refer to the commentary on verse 14 of Surah An-Nahl (Volume 11, page 179 onwards).

After mentioning the blessing of ships, which has a close connection to the daily life of humans, He addresses the issue of the subjection of other beings in general, saying: "And He has subjected to you whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, all from Him" (وَ سَخَّرَ لَكُمْ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً مِنْهُ‌).

He has given you such personality, value, and greatness that He subjected all beings in the universe and placed them in the path of your interests. The sun and the moon, wind and rain, mountains and valleys, forests and deserts, trees and animals, minerals and underground resources—in short, He invited all these beings to your service and made them all bewildered and subservient to you, so that you may benefit from His gifts and not fall into heedlessness.

It is noteworthy that He says: "all from Him" (جميعا منه): "All of these, with all their characteristics and differences, are from Him and at your service by His command." [^1] Considering that all blessings are from His side, and He is the Creator, the Disposer, and the Lord of all, why then should man go to others? Why should he bow at the threshold of weak creatures? And why should he remain ignorant of the knowledge of the True Benefactor?

Therefore, at the end of the verse, He adds: "Indeed in that are signs for a people who give thought" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ‌).

In the previous verse, human emotions were utilized, and here, their intellects and thoughts. What a Merciful God who speaks to His servants in every possible language—sometimes with the language of the heart, and sometimes with the language of thought—and the goal in all of these is but one thing: the awakening of heedless humans and setting them in motion on the journey toward Allah.

Regarding the subjection of various beings of the world, we have provided a detailed discussion under verses 31 to 33 of Surah Ibrahim (Volume 10, page 349 onwards).

[^1]: Regarding the grammatical analysis (I'rab) and composition of "جميعا منه", several possibilities have been suggested: Zamakhshari in Al-Kashshaf gives two possibilities: first, that "جميعا منه" is a circumstantial clause (Hal) for "whatever is in the heavens and the earth," meaning all these are subjected to you while being from Him. Second, that it is the predicate (Khabar) of an omitted subject, estimated as "they are all from Him" (هى منه جميعا). Some have also suggested it might be an emphasis (Ta'kid) for "whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth."

Then He mentions an ethical instruction regarding interaction with the disbelievers to complete the previous logical discussions. Addressing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, He says: "Tell those who believe to forgive those who do not hope for the days of Allah (the Day of Resurrection), and not to be harsh with them" (قُلْ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا يَغْفِرُوا لِلَّذِينَ لا يَرْجُونَ أَيَّامَ اللَّهِ‌).

It is possible that due to being far from the principles of faith and Divine upbringing, they may exhibit harsh and undesirable behaviors, or use ugly and offensive expressions. You must encounter such individuals with nobility and broad-mindedness, lest they increase in their stubbornness and their distance from the truth grows. This good character, forgiveness, and tolerance both reduce their pressure and may serve as a factor to attract them toward faith.

Similar instructions have appeared repeatedly in the verses of the Quran, such as: "So overlook them and say, 'Peace.' But they are going to know" (فَاصْفَحْ عَنْهُمْ وَ قُلْ سَلامٌ فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ‌) (Az-Zukhruf: 89).

In principle, when dealing with ignorant individuals, harshness and insistence on punishment often do not yield the desired result, whereas indifference and nobility toward them are means for awakening and factors for guidance.

Of course, this is not a universal rule, for it cannot be denied that cases arise where there is no choice but firmness and punishment, but these are in the minority.

Another point is that all days are the days of Allah, yet "Ayyam Allah" (the Days of Allah) is applied to specific days because it indicates their importance and greatness.

This expression appears in two places in the Holy Quran: one in the verse under discussion, and the other in a verse of Surah Ibrahim, where it has a broader and more extensive meaning.

In Islamic hadiths, "Ayyam Allah" has been interpreted as various days, including:

In the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim, it is stated that the Days of Allah are three: the day of the uprising of Al-Mahdi عليه السلام, the day of death, and the Day of Resurrection. [^1] In another hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

"The Days of Allah are His blessings, and His trials through calamities, Glory be to Him." [^2] In any case, this expression is a sign of the importance of the Day of Judgment—the day of Allah's manifest sovereignty over everyone and everything, and the day of great justice.

But so that such individuals do not abuse this nobility and forgiveness, He adds at the end of the verse: "This is so that He may recompense a people for what they used to earn" (لِيَجْزِيَ قَوْماً بِما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ‌).

A group of commentators has considered this sentence a threat to the disbelievers and criminals, while others have counted it as a glad tiding for the believers in return for this forgiveness and pardon.

However, there is no obstacle to it being both a threat to that group and a glad tiding to this group, as the following verse also points to this same meaning.

He says: "Whoever does a righteous deed, it is for his own soul; and whoever does evil, it is against it. Then to your Lord you will be returned" and you will find the result of your deeds (مَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً فَلِنَفْسِهِ وَ مَنْ أَساءَ فَعَلَيْها ثُمَّ إِلى‌ رَبِّكُمْ تُرْجَعُونَ‌).

This expression, which appears repeatedly in the verses of the Quran with various wordings, is an answer to those who say: "What benefit or harm does our obedience or rebellion bring to Allah?"

And why is there such insistence regarding obedience and the prohibition of His disobedience?! These verses say: The benefit and harm of all these return to yourselves. It is you who attain perfection in the light of righteous deeds and fly toward the heaven of proximity to Allah, and it is you who fall due to crime and sin into the abyss of His wrath and become afflicted with eternal curse after being distanced from His mercy.

All the programs of obligation, the sending of messengers, and the revelation of books are all for this very purpose.

Therefore, the Quran says in one place: "And whoever is grateful is grateful for [the benefit of] himself. And whoever is ungrateful—then indeed, Allah is Free of need and Praiseworthy" (Luqman: 12).

In another place, it says: "Whoever is guided is only guided for [the benefit of] his soul; and whoever goes astray only goes astray against it" (Az-Zumar: 41). And in another place, we read: "And whoever purifies himself only purifies himself for [the benefit of] his soul. And to Allah is the destination" (Fatir: 18).

In short, such expressions reflect the reality that the call of the "callers to Allah" in all its dimensions is an extensive service to humanity; it is neither a service to Allah, who is independent of everything, nor to His prophets, whose reward is only upon Allah. Attention to this reality is an effective factor for moving toward obedience and avoiding sin.

[^1] and [^2]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Volume 2, page 526.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحُكْمَ وَٱلنُّبُوَّةَ وَرَزَقْنَـٰهُم مِّنَ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ وَفَضَّلْنَـٰهُمْ عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٦)
Certainly We gave the Children of Israel the Book, judgement and prophethood and We provided them with all the good things. We gave them an advantage over all the nations, (16)
وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُم بَيِّنَـٰتٍ مِّنَ ٱلْأَمْرِ ۖ فَمَا ٱخْتَلَفُوٓا۟ إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْعِلْمُ بَغْيًۢا بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِى بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فِيمَا كَانُوا۟ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ (١٧)
and We gave them manifest precepts. But they did not differ except after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves. Indeed your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which they used to differ. (17)
ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَـٰكَ عَلَىٰ شَرِيعَةٍ مِّنَ ٱلْأَمْرِ فَٱتَّبِعْهَا وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْوَآءَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (١٨)
Then We set you upon a clear course of the Law; so follow it, and do not follow the desires of those who do not know. (18)
إِنَّهُمْ لَن يُغْنُوا۟ عَنكَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءُ بَعْضٍ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ وَلِىُّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (١٩)
Indeed they will not avail you in any way against Allah. Indeed the wrongdoers are allies of one another, but Allah is the ally of the Godwary. (19)
هَـٰذَا بَصَـٰٓئِرُ لِلنَّاسِ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ (٢٠)
These are eye-openers for mankind, and guidance and mercy for a people who have certainty. (20)

Commentary

We granted all these blessings to the Children of Israel, yet...

Following the discussions in the previous verses regarding various types of divine blessings, gratitude, and righteous deeds, these verses describe an example from the lives of certain past nations who were encompassed by Allah's blessings but responded with ingratitude.

It says: "We gave the Children of Israel the Scripture, judgment, and prophethood; We provided them with good things and favored them over the worlds (of their time)" (وَ لَقَدْ آتَيْنا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ الْكِتابَ وَ الْحُكْمَ وَ النُّبُوَّةَ وَ رَزَقْناهُمْ مِنَ الطَّيِّباتِ وَ فَضَّلْناهُمْ عَلَى الْعالَمِينَ‌).

In this verse, a total of five blessings granted by Allah to the Children of Israel are mentioned, which, when added to another blessing mentioned in the following verse, total six great favors.

First is the matter of the Heavenly Book, namely the "Torah," which clarified religious knowledge, the lawful and the prohibited, and the paths of guidance and prosperity.

Second is the station of governance and judgment, for we know they possessed a powerful and extensive government for many years; not only Dawud and Sulayman, but a great number of the Children of Israel were powerful rulers in their era.

"Hukm" in Quranic terminology usually means judgment and arbitration, but since the station of judgment is always a part of the administrative program of government—and a judge can achieve nothing without the backing of governmental power—it also implies the matter of leadership and governance.

In verse 44 of Surah Al-Ma'idah, we read regarding the Torah: يَحْكُمُ بِهَا النَّبِيُّونَ الَّذِينَ أَسْلَمُوا: "The prophets who submitted [to Allah] judged by it for the Jews."

The third divine blessing upon them was the station of "Prophethood," as Allah chose many prophets from among them.

In a narration, it is stated: "The number of the prophets of the Children of Israel reached one thousand" [1], and in another narration, the prophets of the Children of Israel are mentioned as being four thousand [2]. All of these were favors from the Lord upon them.

In the fourth stage, it speaks of material blessings in a comprehensive and all-encompassing manner, saying: "We provided them with good things" (وَ رَزَقْناهُمْ مِنَ الطَّيِّباتِ‌).

The fifth and final blessing was their undisputed superiority and power, as it adds at the end of the verse: "And We favored them over the worlds" (وَ فَضَّلْناهُمْ عَلَى الْعالَمِينَ‌).

Undoubtedly, "the worlds" (al-alamin) here refers to the people of that same era, because verse 110 of Surah Al-Imran explicitly states: كُنْتُمْ خَيْرَ أُمَّةٍ أُخْرِجَتْ لِلنَّاسِ‌: "You are the best nation produced [as an example] for mankind."

Furthermore, we know that the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله is the most superior of prophets; therefore, his Ummah is also the superior Ummah, as stated in verse 89 of Surah An-Nahl: وَ يَوْمَ نَبْعَثُ فِي كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ شَهِيداً عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ أَنْفُسِهِمْ وَ جِئْنا بِكَ شَهِيداً عَلى‌ هؤُلاءِ: "And [mention] the Day when We will resurrect among every nation a witness over them from themselves. And We will bring you, [O Muhammad], as a witness over these [your nation]."

[1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 9, p. 75. [2]: "Bihar al-Anwar," New Edition, Vol. 11, p. 31.

In the next verse, it points to the sixth great blessing that Allah gave to this ungrateful people, saying: "And We gave them clear proofs of the matter [of religion]" (وَ آتَيْناهُمْ بَيِّناتٍ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ). "Bayyinat" (clear proofs) may refer to the clear miracles that Allah granted to Musa ibn Imran عليه السلام and other prophets of the Children of Israel, or it may refer to manifest logical proofs and arguments, and firm laws and ordinances.

Some commentators have suggested that this expression refers to the clear signs Allah provided them regarding the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, by which they could recognize the Final Prophet just as they recognized their own sons: "Those to whom We gave the Scripture know him as they know their own sons" (Al-Baqarah: 146).

However, there is no obstacle to all these meanings being combined in the verse.

In any case, despite these great blessings and clear, manifest proofs, there was no room for disagreement. Yet, these ungrateful people soon turned to discord, as the Quran says in the continuation of this verse: "And they did not differ except after knowledge had come to them—out of jealous animosity among themselves" (فَمَا اخْتَلَفُوا إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ بَغْياً بَيْنَهُمْ‌).

Yes, they raised the banner of rebellion, and each group fell upon the other; they even turned the factors of unity and cohesion into means of division and discord. Consequently, their power turned to weakness, the star of their greatness set, their government collapsed, and they became wanderers in the world.

Some have also said this refers to the disagreement they had regarding the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله after having sufficient knowledge of his attributes.

The Quran warns them at the end of the verse with this phrase: "Indeed, your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that over which they used to differ" (إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِي بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فِيما كانُوا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ‌).

Thus, by being ungrateful for blessings and creating discord, they both lost their greatness and power in this world and earned the punishment of the Hereafter for themselves.

After mentioning the blessings given to the Children of Israel which they met with ingratitude, it speaks of a magnificent blessing bestowed upon the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and the Muslims, saying: "Then We put you on an ordained way (Shari'ah) of the matter [of religion]" (ثُمَّ جَعَلْناكَ عَلى‌ شَرِيعَةٍ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ). "Shari'ah" literally means a path created to reach water at the edge of rivers where the water level is lower than the bank. Subsequently, it was applied to any path that leads a person to their destination and goal. The use of this term for the true religion is because it leads man to the source of revelation, divine pleasure, and eternal happiness, which are like the water of life. This word is used once in the Quran and only in relation to Islam.

The meaning of "al-Amr" (the matter) here is the true religion and faith, which was also referred to in the previous verse where it said: (بَيِّناتٍ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ).

Since this path is the path of salvation and victory, it immediately commands the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "So follow it" (فَاتَّبِعْها).

And since the opposite of it is nothing but following the whims and desires of the ignorant, it adds at the end of the verse: "And do not follow the desires of those who do not know" (وَ لا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْواءَ الَّذِينَ لا يَعْلَمُونَ‌).

In reality, there are only two paths: "the path of the prophets and revelation" and "the path of the whims and desires of the ignorant." If someone turns their back on the first, they will fall into the second path, and if someone turns toward the latter, they will be separated from the line of the prophets. In this way, the Quran draws a line of cancellation over any program of guidance that does not draw support from the source of revelation.

It is noteworthy that some commentators have said: The leaders of the Quraysh came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said, "Come and return to the religion of your ancestors, for they were both more virtuous than you and older!" At that time, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was still in Mecca; the above verse was revealed [1] and answered them that the way to reach the truth is the heavenly revelation sent down to you, not what the whims of these ignorant people dictate.

True leaders, when presenting a new, pure, and modern faith, have always faced this insinuation from the ignorant: "Do you understand better, or your ancestors and the great predecessors before you?" They insisted that they continue those same superstitious methods; had such proposals been acted upon, humanity would never have taken a single step toward perfection.

[1]: Tafsir al-Kabir, "Fakhr al-Razi," Vol. 27, p. 265.

The next verse is, in fact, a reason for the prohibition of submitting to the proposals of the polytheists, saying: "Indeed, they will never avail you against Allah at all" (إِنَّهُمْ لَنْ يُغْنُوا عَنْكَ مِنَ اللَّهِ شَيْئاً). If you were to follow their false religion and divine punishment were to seize you, they could never rush to your aid; or if Allah were to deprive you of a blessing, they would not be able to compensate for it. Although the address in these verses is to the Prophet, the intended audience is all believers.

Then it adds: "Indeed, the wrongdoers are allies of one another" (إِنَّ الظَّالِمِينَ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِياءُ بَعْضٍ‌). They are all of the same cloth, on the same path, and part of the same fabric; and all are weak and powerless.

But do not think that you and the believers, who are currently in the minority, have no helper: "And Allah is the protector of the righteous" (وَ اللَّهُ وَلِيُّ الْمُتَّقِينَ‌). It is true that they apparently have a vast population and significant power and wealth, but in the face of the infinite power of Truth, they are nothing more than an insignificant atom.

In the last verse under discussion, as an emphasis on what has passed and an invitation to follow this divine religion, it says: "This [Quran] is enlightenment for mankind and guidance and mercy for a people who are certain [in faith]" (هذا بَصائِرُ لِلنَّاسِ وَ هُدىً وَ رَحْمَةٌ لِقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ‌). "Basa'ir" is the plural of "basirah," meaning insight or vision. Although this word is mostly used regarding intellectual and rational insights, it is sometimes applied to all matters that serve as a means of perceiving and understanding a subject.

It is interesting that it says "This [Quran and Shari'ah] is insights," meaning it is the essence of insight itself—and not just one insight, but many; not in one dimension, but it gives man correct vision in all dimensions of life.

A similar expression appears in other verses of the Quran, including verse 104 of Surah Al-An'am: قَدْ جاءَكُمْ بَصائِرُ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ‌: "Insights have come to you from your Lord."

Three subjects are raised in this verse: "insights," "guidance," and "mercy," which are respectively the cause and effect of one another. The enlightening verses and the vision-granting Shari'ah lead man toward guidance, and guidance is the source of the Lord's mercy.

It is noteworthy that it mentions "insights" for all people (mankind), but specifies "guidance" and "mercy" for those who possess certainty. This must be so, because the verses of the Quran are not restricted to a specific nation or group; rather, all humans included in the concept of "al-Nas" (mankind) share in it, without any limitation of time or place. However, naturally, guidance is a branch of certainty, and divine mercy is born from it, and it does not encompass everyone.

In any case, saying that the Quran is the essence of insight, guidance, and mercy is a beautiful expression that recounts the greatness, influence, and depth of this Heavenly Book for those who are travelers on the path and seekers of truth.

Ayahs

أَمْ حَسِبَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱجْتَرَحُوا۟ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ أَن نَّجْعَلَهُمْ كَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ سَوَآءً مَّحْيَاهُمْ وَمَمَاتُهُمْ ۚ سَآءَ مَا يَحْكُمُونَ (٢١)
Do those who have perpetrated misdeeds suppose that We shall treat them like those who have faith and do righteous deeds, their life and death being equal? Evil is the judgement that they make! (21)
وَخَلَقَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَلِتُجْزَىٰ كُلُّ نَفْسٍۭ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (٢٢)
Allah created the heavens and the earth with consummate wisdom, so that every soul may be requited for what it has earned, and they will not be wronged. (22)
أَفَرَءَيْتَ مَنِ ٱتَّخَذَ إِلَـٰهَهُۥ هَوَىٰهُ وَأَضَلَّهُ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ عِلْمٍ وَخَتَمَ عَلَىٰ سَمْعِهِۦ وَقَلْبِهِۦ وَجَعَلَ عَلَىٰ بَصَرِهِۦ غِشَـٰوَةً فَمَن يَهْدِيهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ أَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ (٢٣)
Have you seen him who has taken his desire to be his god and whom Allah has led astray knowingly, set a seal upon his hearing and heart, and put a blindfold on his sight? So who will guide him after Allah [has consigned him to error]? Will you not then take admonition? (23)

Commentary

The Life and Death of These Two Groups Are Not Equal

Following the previous verses which spoke of two groups—the "believers" and the "disbelievers," or the "pious" and the "criminals"—the first verse under discussion places these two in a fundamental comparison, stating: "Do those who commit evil deeds think that We shall hold them as equal to those who believe and do righteous deeds, so that their life and their death shall be the same?" (أَمْ حَسِبَ الَّذِينَ اجْتَرَحُوا السَّيِّئاتِ أَنْ نَجْعَلَهُمْ كَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ سَواءً مَحْياهُمْ وَ مَماتُهُمْ‌).

"Evil is the judgment they make!" (ساءَ ما يَحْكُمُونَ‌).

Is it possible for light and darkness, knowledge and ignorance, good and evil, or faith and disbelief to be equal? Is it possible for the reflection, fruit, and result of these unequal matters to be equal?

Never is it so; the believers who perform righteous deeds are distinct from the faithless criminals in everything. Faith, disbelief, and good or evil deeds color the entirety of the life and death of each group accordingly.

This verse is similar to verse 28 of Surah Sad, which says: "Shall We treat those who believe and do righteous deeds as those who spread corruption on earth? Or shall We treat the pious as the wicked?" (أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ كَالْمُفْسِدِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الْمُتَّقِينَ كَالْفُجَّارِ).

Or like verses 35 and 36 of Surah Al-Qalam, which state: "Shall We then treat the Muslims like the criminals? What is [the matter] with you? How do you judge?" (أَ فَنَجْعَلُ الْمُسْلِمِينَ كَالْمُجْرِمِينَ ما لَكُمْ كَيْفَ تَحْكُمُونَ‌).

The word ijtarahu (اجترحوا) is derived from the root jarh (جرح), which originally means a wound or an effect caused by illness or injury to the human body. Since committing a sin is as if it wounds one's soul, the term ijtiraah is also used to mean committing a sin. Sometimes it is used in a broader sense, meaning any kind of acquisition or earning. The limbs of the body are called jawarih (جوارح) because through them, a person carries out his purposes and acquires or earns what he desires.

In any case, this verse states: it is a false notion to imagine that faith and righteous deeds, or disbelief and sin, have no impact on human life. It is not so; the life and death of these two groups are completely different.

Believers, in the light of faith and righteous deeds, enjoy a specific tranquility such that even the hardest events of life do not affect their souls. In contrast, faithless and tainted individuals are constantly in anxiety. If they are in blessing, the fear of its loss continuously torments them; if they are in calamity and distress, they lack the power to confront it. As we read in verse 82 of Surah Al-An'am: "Those who believe and do not obscure their faith with wrongdoing—it is they who shall have security, and they are the [rightly] guided." (الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ لَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمانَهُمْ بِظُلْمٍ أُولئِكَ لَهُمُ الْأَمْنُ وَ هُمْ مُهْتَدُونَ‌).

People of faith are encouraged by Divine promises and are subject to His special favors, as we read in verse 51 of Surah Al-Mu'min: "Indeed, We will support Our messengers and those who believe during the life of this world and on the Day when the witnesses will stand." (إِنَّا لَنَنْصُرُ رُسُلَنا وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَ يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْأَشْهادُ).

The light of guidance illuminates the hearts of the first group, and they advance with firm steps toward their sacred goal: "Allah is the Ally of those who believe. He brings them out from darknesses into the light." (اللَّهُ وَلِيُّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا يُخْرِجُهُمْ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ) (Al-Baqarah, 257).

However, the second group finds neither a specific goal for life nor a clear program, and they wander amidst waves of darkness: "And those who disbelieve—their allies are Taghut; they take them out of the light into darknesses." (وَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَوْلِياؤُهُمُ الطَّاغُوتُ يُخْرِجُونَهُمْ مِنَ النُّورِ إِلَى الظُّلُماتِ‌).

This pertains to the life of this world. As for the moment of death, which is a window to the eternal realm and a gate to the Hereafter, as the Quran says in verse 32 of Surah Al-Nahl: "Those whom the angels take in death while they are pure; they [the angels] say, 'Peace be upon you. Enter Paradise for what you used to do.'" (الَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ طَيِّبِينَ يَقُولُونَ سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ ادْخُلُوا الْجَنَّةَ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ‌).

Meanwhile, the faithless criminals are addressed differently, as stated in verses 28 and 29 of the same Surah Al-Nahl: "Those whom the angels take in death while they are wronging themselves. Then they will offer submission, [saying], 'We were not doing any evil.' Yes! Indeed, Allah is Knowing of what you used to do. So enter the gates of Hell to abide eternally therein, and wretched is the residence of the arrogant." (الَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ ظالِمِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ فَأَلْقَوُا السَّلَمَ ما كُنَّا نَعْمَلُ مِنْ سُوءٍ بَلى‌ إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ- فَادْخُلُوا أَبْوابَ جَهَنَّمَ خالِدِينَ فيها فَلَبِئْسَ مَثْوَى الْمُتَكَبِّرِينَ‌).

In short, the difference between these two groups exists in all aspects of life, death, the world of Barzakh, and the Resurrection. [^1]

The next verse is, in fact, a commentary and a justification for the previous verse, stating: "Allah created the heavens and the earth in truth" (وَ خَلَقَ اللَّهُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ بِالْحَقِ‌). "And so that every soul may be recompensed for what it has earned, and they will not be wronged." (وَ لِتُجْزى‌ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِما كَسَبَتْ وَ هُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ‌).

The entire universe shows that the Creator of this world has established it upon the axis of truth, and truth and justice prevail everywhere.

Given this, how could it be possible for Him to treat the righteous believers and the faithless criminals as equals, making such a thing an exception to the law of creation?

Naturally, those who move in harmony with this law of truth and justice must benefit from the blessings of existence and Divine favors, while those who step against it must become prey to the burning fire of God's wrath and anger; justice demands exactly this.

[^1]: Other possibilities have been suggested by commentators regarding the above verse, including that the phrase "سَواءً مَحْياهُمْ وَ مَماتُهُمْ‌" means that for the faithless criminals, their life and death are the same—they have no goodness, blessing, or obedience in life, nor in death; they are alive but like the dead (thus both pronouns refer to the criminals). Another view is that "life" refers to the life on the Day of Resurrection, meaning that believers and disbelievers are not equal at the time of death and when being raised in the Resurrection. However, the apparent meaning of the verse is what we have provided above. From this, it becomes clear that "justice" does not mean "absolute equality," but rather justice is that everyone benefits from blessings according to their merits.

The last verse under discussion provides another explanation and justification for the lack of equality between disbelievers and believers, stating: "Have you seen him who has taken his own desire as his god?" (أَ فَرَأَيْتَ مَنِ اتَّخَذَ إِلهَهُ هَواهُ‌). "And Allah has led him astray despite [his] knowledge" (وَ أَضَلَّهُ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ عِلْمٍ‌).

"And has set a seal upon his hearing and his heart and put over his vision a veil," so that he remains forever wandering in the valley of misguidance (وَ خَتَمَ عَلى‌ سَمْعِهِ وَ قَلْبِهِ وَ جَعَلَ عَلى‌ بَصَرِهِ غِشاوَةً).

"Who then will guide him after Allah?" (فَمَنْ يَهْدِيهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ اللَّهِ‌).

"Will you not then take admonition?" And do you not understand the difference between such a person and those who have found their way in the light of truth? (أَ فَلا تَذَكَّرُونَ‌).

Here, the question arises: how is it possible for a person to make his own desires his deity?

It is clear that when one abandons God's command and follows the heart's whims and desires, prioritizing obedience to them over obedience to the Truth, this is the very worship of desires, for one of the well-known meanings of "worship" (ibadah) is obedience.

As it has appeared many times in the Holy Quran regarding Satan or the rabbis and scholars of the Jews, that "a group worships Satan" (Ya-Sin, 60), and regarding the Jews, it says: "They have taken their scholars as lords besides Allah" (At-Tawbah, 31).

In a Hadith, it is narrated that Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام said: "By Allah, they (the Jews and Christians) did not fast for them, nor did they pray to them, but they [the leaders] made lawful for them what was forbidden and forbade for them what was lawful, so they followed them and thus worshipped them without even realizing it!" [^1]

However, some commentators consider this expression to refer to the idolaters of Quraysh, who would become attached to anything and make an idol out of it to worship, and whenever they found another object that caught their attention, they would discard the first idol and make one from the second! Thus, their deity was whatever their desires approved of. [^2] But the expression "he who has taken his desire as his god" is more consistent with the first interpretation.

Regarding the phrase "Allah has led him astray despite [his] knowledge," the well-known interpretation is what we mentioned above: "Allah, knowing that they do not deserve guidance, has led them astray," indicating that they have broken all the lamps of guidance with their own hands, closed the paths of salvation upon themselves, and destroyed the bridges of return behind them. In such conditions, Allah withdraws His grace and mercy from them and strips them of the sense to distinguish good from evil, as if He has placed their hearts and ears in a container, closed and sealed them, and cast a heavy veil over their eyes.

These are, in fact, the effects of what they have chosen for themselves and the grim result of the deity they have selected.

Truly, what a dangerous idol is the worship of desires, which closes all doors of mercy and paths of salvation to man! How eloquent and meaningful is the Hadith narrated from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله: "No deity worshipped under the sky is more hated by Allah than the desire (hawa)!" [^1]

However, some commentators have said: this phrase indicates that these stubborn ego-worshippers choose the path of misguidance despite having knowledge and awareness of the path of guidance, for knowledge is not always accompanied by guidance, and misguidance is not always accompanied by ignorance.

Knowledge is a source of guidance only when a person is committed to its requirements and walks alongside it to reach the destination. As the Quran says regarding a group of stubborn disbelievers: "And they rejected them, though their inner selves were convinced thereof" (An-Naml, 14). [^2] But the first interpretation is more appropriate, considering that the pronouns in the verse refer to Allah, as He says He has led him astray and sealed his hearing and heart.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 209. [^2]: Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 35.

From what we have said, it is clearly evident that there is no sign of fatalism (jabr) in the verse; rather, it is an emphasis on the principle of free will and the determination of human destiny by one's own hand.

Regarding Allah setting a seal on the heart and hearing of man and casting a veil over his heart, we have provided more extensive discussions in Volume 1 under verse 7 of Surah Al-Baqarah. [^1]

Key Points

1- The Most Dangerous of Idols is the Idol of Desires We read in the Hadith that the most hated deity worshipped is the idol of whims and desires. There is no exaggeration in this statement, for ordinary idols are useless objects, but the idol of whims and desires is seductive and drives one toward all kinds of sin and deviation. Generally, it can be said that this idol has characteristics that make it deserve the title "the most loathsome of idols." It beautifies ugliness in the eyes of man, to the point where man boasts of his evil deeds and, as an example of "while they think that they are doing good" (Al-Kahf, 104), prides himself on them as a righteous person!

2- The Most Effective Way for Satan's Influence is Ego-Worship For as long as there is no base within a person, Satan has no power to whisper, and Satan's base is nothing but ego-worship—the very thing for which Satan himself fell and was expelled from the ranks of the angels and the station of Divine proximity.

3- Ego-Worship It strips man of the most important means of guidance, which is the correct perception of realities, and casts a veil over the eyes and intellect of man, as explicitly mentioned in the verses under discussion after the issue of ego-worship. Other verses of the Quran also testify to this reality.

4- Ego-Worship Leads Man to the Stage of Battling God (God forbid) Just as the leader of the ego-worshippers, namely Satan, met such a grim fate and objected to Allah's wisdom regarding the command to prostrate to Adam, deeming it unwise!

5- The Consequences of Ego-Worship They are so grim and painful that sometimes a moment of ego-worship brings a lifetime of regret, and sometimes a moment of ego-worship blows away the product of a person's entire life and the merits of his righteous deeds. Therefore, this matter is emphasized and warned against in the verses of the Quran and Islamic narrations.

In a well-known Hadith of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated: "The thing I fear most for my Ummah is ego-worship and long-held hopes; as for ego-worship, it bars one from the truth, and as for long-held hopes, they cause the Hereafter to be forgotten!" [^1]

And in a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, we read that in response to the question: "Which oppressive ruler is the most dominant and powerful?" He replied: "Desire (Al-Hawa)." [^2]

[^1]: Vol. 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh, p. 81 onwards, under verse 7 of Surah Al-Baqarah. [^1] and [^2]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 70, pp. 75, 76, and 77.

In another Hadith from Imam Zayn al-Abidin عليه السلام, it is stated that Allah says: "By My Might and My Majesty, and My Glory and My Splendor, and My High Station and the Loftiness of My Place, no servant prefers My desire over his own desire except that I place his concern in his Hereafter, and his richness in his heart, and I take care of his affairs, and I guarantee his sustenance from the heavens and the earth, and the world comes to him submissively!" [^1]

And in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated: "Beware of your desires as you beware of your enemies, for there is nothing more inimical to men than following their desires and the harvests of their tongues." [^2]

Finally, in another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, he said: "I indeed hope for salvation for this Ummah for those among them who recognize our right, except for one of three: a companion of an oppressive ruler, a follower of desire, and an open sinner (who has no shame)." [^3]

In this regard, verses and narrations are very numerous and rich.

We conclude this discussion with a meaningful statement that some have narrated as a reason for revelation (shan-i nuzul), which is a living witness to our point. One of the commentators says: One night, Abu Jahl, accompanied by Walid ibn al-Mughirah, performed the circumambulation (tawaf) of the Kaaba. During the tawaf, they spoke about the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. Abu Jahl said: "By Allah, I know that he is truthful!" Immediately, Walid said to him: "Hush! Why do you say this?"

Abu Jahl replied: "O Walid, we used to call him the Truthful and Trustworthy (Sadiq al-Amin) in his childhood and youth; how can we call him a liar and a traitor after his intellect has matured and his growth is complete? I repeat: I know he is truthful!" Walid asked: "Then why do you not confirm him and believe?"

He said: "Do you want the daughters of Quraysh to sit and say that out of fear of defeat, I surrendered to the nephew of Abu Talib? By the idols of Lat and Uzza, I will never follow him!" It was then that the verse "And Allah has set a seal upon his hearing and his heart" was revealed. [^1]

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 70, pp. 75, 76, and 77. [^2]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, Chapter on Following Desires, Hadith 1. [^3]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 70, p. 76. [^1]: Tafsir al-Maraghi, Vol. 25, p. 27.

Ayahs

وَقَالُوا۟ مَا هِىَ إِلَّا حَيَاتُنَا ٱلدُّنْيَا نَمُوتُ وَنَحْيَا وَمَا يُهْلِكُنَآ إِلَّا ٱلدَّهْرُ ۚ وَمَا لَهُم بِذَٰلِكَ مِنْ عِلْمٍ ۖ إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَظُنُّونَ (٢٤)
They say, ‘There is nothing but the life of this world: we live and we die, and nothing but time destroys us.’ But they do not have any knowledge of that, and they only make conjectures. (24)
وَإِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتُنَا بَيِّنَـٰتٍ مَّا كَانَ حُجَّتَهُمْ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوا۟ ٱئْتُوا۟ بِـَٔابَآئِنَآ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٢٥)
When Our manifest signs are recited to them, their only argument is to say, ‘Bring our fathers back [to life], if you are truthful.’ (25)

Commentary

The Beliefs of the Dahris (Materialists)

In these verses, there is another discussion regarding the deniers of Monotheism (Tawhid); however, here it specifically mentions a particular group of them known as the "Dahris" (Materialists), who absolutely denied the existence of a Wise Creator in the universe. This is while most polytheists apparently believed in God and considered idols to be intercessors before Him. It says: "And they said: There is nothing but our life in this world; some of us die and some are born," and take their place, and thus the human race continues (وَ قالُوا ما هِيَ إِلَّا حَياتُنَا الدُّنْيا نَمُوتُ وَ نَحْيا).

"And nothing destroys us but Dahr (time/the passage of ages)" (وَ ما يُهْلِكُنا إِلَّا الدَّهْرُ).

In this way, they denied both the "Resurrection" and the "Origin" (the Creator). The first sentence refers to the denial of the Resurrection, and the subsequent sentence refers to the denial of the Origin.

It is noteworthy that similar expressions appear in two other verses of the Quran. In Surah Al-An'am, verse 29, we read: وَ قالُوا إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا حَياتُنَا الدُّنْيا وَ ما نَحْنُ بِمَبْعُوثِينَ and in Surah Al-Mu'minun, verse 37, it says: إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا حَياتُنَا الدُّنْيا نَمُوتُ وَ نَحْيا وَ ما نَحْنُ بِمَبْعُوثِينَ. However, in both those instances, the emphasis is on the denial of the Resurrection, whereas in the verse under discussion, both the Resurrection and the Origin are denied.

It is clear that their greater emphasis on the Resurrection was due to the terror they felt toward it, and the impact it could have on changing the course of their lustful lives.

Commentators have mentioned several interpretations for the phrase "نموت و نحيا" (we die and we live): First, as mentioned above: the elders pass away, and newborns enter the realm of life and take their place. Second: the sentence involves a transposition (hysteron proteron), meaning: we live and then we die, and there is nothing else besides this life and death! Third: some die and some remain alive (though eventually all will die). Fourth: we were initially dead and lifeless, then we were clothed in the garment of life. However, the first interpretation is the most appropriate of all.

In any case, the belief that the agent behind the events of this world is Dahr (time/fate), or as some others expressed it, the rotation of the celestial spheres and the positions of the stars, was the belief of a group of materialists in past ages who traced the chain of events back to the heavens. They believed that whatever happens in our world is caused by them [1]. Even a group of Dahri philosophers and their like believed in the existence of intellect for the celestial spheres and considered the management of this world to be in their hands.

These superstitious beliefs gradually vanished over time, especially with the advancement of astronomy, which proved that something called "spheres" (concentric crystalline layers like onion skins) has no external existence at all. The stars in the upper world also have structures more or less like the planet Earth; some are dark and receive light from other bodies, while others are in a state of combustion and radiance.

The Dahris would sometimes speak ill of Dahr during bitter and unpleasant events, reviling and insulting it. Surprisingly, remnants of this are still seen in today's literature, where some God-fearing poets speak ill of "treacherous time" (dahr-e ghaddar) and the "crooked-moving wheel" (charkh-e kaj-madar), casting curses upon the times for why things happened as they did.

In a famous poem it is said: The heavens give the reins of desire to the ignorant; You are a person of knowledge and virtue—this sin is enough for you!

Another says: It is the way of the times to sometimes grant honor and sometimes humiliation; The playful wheel has many such playthings!

Regarding Dahr, it has also been said: When Dahr practices trickery and the wheel acts with deceit, It agitates the brain and bewilders the intellect!

However, in Islamic hadiths, it is narrated from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله: "Do not revile Dahr (time), for Allah is Dahr." [1] This indicates that Dahr is nothing more than a word; the One who is the Manager of this world and the Controller of this universe is Allah. If you speak ill of the Manager and Controller of this world, you have spoken ill of the Almighty Allah without realizing it! A clear witness to this statement is another hadith narrated as a Hadith Qudsi, where Allah says: "The son of Adam harms Me by reviling Dahr, while I am Dahr! In My hand is the command; I alternate the night and the day!" [2]

However, in some expressions, Dahr is used to mean "the people of the age" and "the people of the times," about whose unfaithfulness great figures have complained. This is similar to the famous poem narrated from Imam Hussain عليه السلام on the night of Ashura, where he said: O Dahr! Fie upon you as a friend! How many, at dawn and dusk, Of companions and seekers have you slain, And Dahr is never satisfied with a substitute!

Thus, there are two meanings for Dahr: Dahr meaning "the heavens and time," which was the focus of the Dahris who imagined it to be the ruler over the system of existence and human lives; and Dahr meaning "the people of the era and time."

Certainly, Dahr in the first sense is nothing but a fantasy, or if it exists, it is an error in terminology where they use the name "Dahr" instead of the name of Allah the Almighty, who has sovereignty over the entire world of existence. But Dahr in the second sense is something that many leaders and great figures have censured, because they found the people of their age to be deceitful, unfaithful, and fickle.

In any case, the Holy Quran has provided a short and profound response to these idle talkers, which is also seen in other parts of the Quran. It says: "They have no certainty in this claim that there is no Resurrection and that the origin of the world is Dahr; rather, they only hold a baseless conjecture" (وَ ما لَهُمْ بِذلِكَ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَظُنُّونَ).

A similar meaning appears in Surah An-Najm, verse 28, regarding those who imagined angels to be the daughters of God: وَ ما لَهُمْ بِهِ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِنْ يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا الظَّنَّ وَ إِنَّ الظَّنَّ لا يُغْنِي مِنَ الْحَقِّ شَيْئاً: "They have no certainty in this; they only follow baseless conjecture, and conjecture in no way avails against the truth."

The same meaning is mentioned regarding the attribution of killing to Prophet Jesus عليه السلام (An-Nisa, 157) and the belief of the Arab polytheists regarding idols (Yunus, 66).

And this is the simplest argument mentioned against such individuals: that you have no logical witness or evidence to prove your claim; you rely only on conjecture and the estimation of fantasies.

In the following verse, it points to one of the baseless excuses of this group regarding the Resurrection, saying: "And when Our clear and manifest signs are recited to them, their only argument against them is that they say: 'Bring back our forefathers if you are truthful'" (وَ إِذا تُتْلى‌ عَلَيْهِمْ آياتُنا بَيِّناتٍ ما كانَ حُجَّتَهُمْ إِلَّا أَنْ قالُوا ائْتُوا بِآبائِنا إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ) [1]. They claimed that if the raising of the dead is true, then as an example, bring our ancestors back to life so we may see and believe, and ask them what news there is after death? Will they confirm your words?! Yes, this was their only argument—a weak and hollow argument, for Allah has shown His power to revive the dead to humanity through various ways:

The first creation of man from dust, the wondrous transformations of the embryo in the womb, the creation of the vast heavens and earth, and the revival of dead lands after the descent of rain—all of which are mentioned in the verses of the Quran as living documents of the possibility of the Resurrection—are the best proofs for this. What need is there for anything else in this regard?

Furthermore, they had practically shown that they had no goal other than making excuses, and even if such a scene were to take place before their eyes, they would immediately say, "This is magic," just as they said in similar instances.

The use of the term "hujjah" (argument/proof) regarding this baseless statement of theirs is, in fact, an allusion to the fact that they have no argument except for a lack of argument.
