[1] Some have suggested a fifth possibility in interpreting this phrase: that it refers to the belief in reincarnation (tanasukh) which a group of idolaters accepted, saying we constantly die and then are revived in other bodies in this same world. However, this interpretation is not consistent with the phrase "وَ ما يُهْلِكُنا إِلَّا الدَّهْرُ" which speaks only of destruction (reflect on this).

[1] Tafsir "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 9, p. 78. [2] Tafsir "Qurtubi," Vol. 9, p. 5991. [1] "Hujjatahum" in the above verse is the predicate (khabar) of "kana" and "an qalu..." is its subject (ism).

Ayahs

قُلِ ٱللَّهُ يُحْيِيكُمْ ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ ثُمَّ يَجْمَعُكُمْ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٢٦)
Say, ‘It is Allah who gives you life, then He makes you die. Then He will gather you on the Day of Resurrection, in which there is no doubt. But most people do not know.’ (26)
وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ ٱلسَّاعَةُ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَخْسَرُ ٱلْمُبْطِلُونَ (٢٧)
To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and when the Hour sets in, the falsifiers will be losers on that day. (27)
وَتَرَىٰ كُلَّ أُمَّةٍ جَاثِيَةً ۚ كُلُّ أُمَّةٍ تُدْعَىٰٓ إِلَىٰ كِتَـٰبِهَا ٱلْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٢٨)
And you will see every nation fallen on its knees. Every nation will be summoned to its book: ‘Today you will be requited for what you used to do. (28)
هَـٰذَا كِتَـٰبُنَا يَنطِقُ عَلَيْكُم بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ إِنَّا كُنَّا نَسْتَنسِخُ مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٢٩)
This is Our book, which speaks truly against you. Indeed We used to record what you used to do.’ (29)
فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ فَيُدْخِلُهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ فِى رَحْمَتِهِۦ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (٣٠)
As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, their Lord will admit them into His mercy. That is the manifest success! (30)
وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَفَلَمْ تَكُنْ ءَايَـٰتِى تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ فَٱسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ وَكُنتُمْ قَوْمًا مُّجْرِمِينَ (٣١)
But as for the faithless, [they will be asked,] ‘Were not My signs recited to you? But you were disdainful, and you were a guilty lot. (31)

Commentary

In That Court of Justice, Everyone Shall Fall to Their Knees!

These verses are, in fact, another response to the claims of the materialists (Dahriyyin) who denied both the Origin and the Resurrection, and whose words were referenced in the preceding verses.

First, He says: "Say: Allah gives you life, then causes you to die; then He will gather you for the Day of Resurrection, about which there is no doubt" (قُلِ اللَّهُ يُحْيِيكُمْ ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ ثُمَّ يَجْمَعُكُمْ إِلى‌ يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ‌).

They accepted neither God nor the Day of Recompense, and the content of this verse serves as an argument for both parts. This is because it emphasizes the initial phenomenon of life; in other words, they could not deny the very existence of the first life and the emergence of living beings from inanimate matter. On one hand, this is a proof for the existence of a Universal Intellect and Knowledge. Is it possible for the phenomenon of life—with its wondrous order, complex secrets, and diverse forms that leave the intellects of all scientists bewildered—to come into existence without the presence of an All-Powerful and All-Knowing God?

For this reason, various verses of the Quran emphasize the phenomenon of life as one of the signs of Monotheism (Tawhid).

On the other hand, He who is capable of the first life, how could He not be capable of restoring it?

As for the expression "no doubt in it" (لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ‌) regarding the Resurrection—where He informs of its occurrence rather than its mere possibility—it may be an allusion to the Law of Divine Justice. This is because, certainly, in this world, not all rightful claimants receive their due, and not all transgressors and oppressors face their punishment. If there were no Court of Justice on the Day of Resurrection, the Justice of the Lord would have no meaning.

And since many people do not reflect or ponder upon these proofs, He adds at the end of the verse: "But most people do not know" (وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ‌).

One of the names of the Resurrection mentioned in the above verse is "The Day of Gathering" (Yawm al-Jam'), because all of creation, from the first to the last, and all classes and types of humans, will all be gathered in one place on that day (this expression also appears in several other verses of the Quran, including Ash-Shura: 7 and At-Taghabun: 9).

The next verse provides another proof for the issue of Resurrection, the premise of which we have read in other verses of the Quran. It says: "To Allah belongs the sovereignty of the heavens and the earth" (وَ لِلَّهِ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ‌). He who is the Owner and Sovereign over the entire expanse of the universe certainly has the power to revive the dead, and such a task is never difficult for His power.

He has made this world a field for the Resurrection and a highly profitable marketplace for the world after death. Therefore, at the end of the verse, He adds: "And on the Day the Hour is established, on that Day the followers of falsehood shall lose" (وَ يَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَخْسَرُ الْمُبْطِلُونَ‌).

This is because they have lost the capital of their lives, conducted no [beneficial] trade, and "purchased no commodity except regret and sorrow."

Life, intellect, intelligence, and the blessings of existence are man's capital in this market of trade. The followers of falsehood exchange them for a fleeting commodity that holds no value in comparison. On the Day of Resurrection, when only a sound heart, faith, and righteous deeds are of use, they will witness their own loss with their own eyes.

"Yakhsar" (يخسر) is derived from "khusran" (loss), meaning the loss of capital. According to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, it is sometimes attributed to the person himself, where it is said: "So-and-so lost" (خسر فلان), and sometimes it is attributed to his trade, where it is said: "His trade lost" (خسرت تجارته). Although the people of this world use this term only regarding wealth, status, and material blessings, more significant than material loss is the loss of the capital of intellect, faith, and spiritual reward.

"Mubtil" (مبطل) is derived from "ibtal" (nullification). In linguistics, it has various meanings: nullifying something, lying, joking and mocking, or presenting a false matter. All these meanings are applicable to the above verse.

Those who nullified the truth, those who promoted false ideologies and beliefs, and those who lied against the Prophets of Allah and mocked their words will see their loss and ruin on that day.

The next verse depicts the scene of the Resurrection in a very vivid manner, saying:

"And you will see every nation kneeling" (وَ تَرى‌ كُلَّ أُمَّةٍ جاثِيَةً).

From some expressions found in the words of great commentators, it is understood that in the past, litigants would sit in this manner before judges to be distinguished from others. On the Day of Resurrection, everyone will likewise sit on their knees in that great Court so that their trial may take place.

It is also possible that this expression indicates their readiness to accept any command or judgment, as individuals in a state of readiness sit on their knees.

Or, it may refer to the weakness, helplessness, fear, and terror that overwhelms them (it is possible for all these meanings to be combined within the concept of the verse).

"Jathiyah" (جاثية) has other meanings as well, including "a dense and crowded multitude" or "group by group," and it could refer to the crowding of humans in the Court of Divine Justice, or the sitting of each nation and group separately. However, the first meaning is more famous and appropriate.

Then, it describes the second scene of the Resurrection scenes as follows:

"Every nation will be called to its record [and it will be said to them]: 'Today you are being recompensed for what you used to do'" (كُلُّ أُمَّةٍ تُدْعى‌ إِلى‌ كِتابِهَا الْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ‌).

This "book" is the record of deeds in which all good and evil, the ugly and the beautiful of human words and actions, are recorded. As the Quran says: "It leaves out nothing small or great except that it has counted it" (لايُغادِرُ صَغِيرَةً وَ لا كَبِيرَةً إِلَّا أَحْصاها) (Al-Kahf: 49).

The expression "Every nation will be called to its record" indicates that in addition to the record of deeds that exists separately for every individual, every nation also has a record of deeds belonging to its collective and group. This does not seem strange considering that man possesses two types of actions: "individual actions" and "collective or group actions." The existence of two types of records of deeds is entirely natural from this perspective. [^1] The term "tud'a" (is called) shows that they are invited to read their statement and record of deeds. This is similar to what is mentioned in verse 14 of Surah Al-Isra: "Read your record. Sufficient is yourself against you this Day as an accountant" (اقْرَأْ كِتابَكَ كَفى‌ بِنَفْسِكَ الْيَوْمَ عَلَيْكَ حَسِيباً).

Once again, they are addressed by Allah, and as an emphasis, He says: "This is Our record which speaks against you with truth; indeed, We were recording what you used to do" (هذا كِتابُنا يَنْطِقُ عَلَيْكُمْ بِالْحَقِ‌).

On that day when you did whatever you wished, you never believed that your actions were being recorded anywhere, but "We had ordered that all the actions you performed be written down" (إِنَّا كُنَّا نَسْتَنْسِخُ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ‌).

"Nastansikh" (نستنسخ) is from the root "istinsakh," which is originally derived from "naskh," meaning to eliminate something by means of something else. For example, it is said: "The sun erased (nasakhat) the shadow."

Later, it came to be used for copying a book from another book without the first book being destroyed.

[^1]: Some commentators have suggested that "the book" in the above verse refers to the "Divine Scripture" revealed to that nation. However, the apparent meaning of the verse, especially in light of the following verse, is the record of deeds, and most commentators hold this view.

Here, a question arises: if Allah ordered that human actions be "copied" (istinsakh), there must have been a book before it from which the record of deeds was written. Therefore, some believe that the record of deeds for all humans was previously written in the Preserved Tablet (Al-Lawh al-Mahfuz), and the angels guarding human deeds copy it from the Preserved Tablet.

However, this meaning is not very consistent with the verse under discussion. What is appropriate is one of two meanings: either "istinsakh" here means the original writing (as some commentators have said), or human actions themselves are like a cosmic book from which the guardian angels of deeds make copies or "photographs." Therefore, in other verses of the Quran, the term "writing" (kitabah) is used instead of this expression. In verse 12 of Surah Ya-Sin, we read: "Indeed, it is We who bring the dead to life and record what they have sent before them and their footprints" (إِنَّا نَحْنُ نُحْيِ الْمَوْتى‌ وَ نَكْتُبُ ما قَدَّمُوا وَ آثارَهُمْ). [^1] Regarding the types of books for recording deeds (personal records, records of nations, and the comprehensive general book of all humans), more discussion is provided under verse 12 of Surah Ya-Sin (Volume 18, page 333).

[^1]: In a narration from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, it is stated: "Indeed, Allah has angels who descend every day to write the deeds of the children of Adam." Sheikh Tusi in Al-Tibyan, under the verse in question, after quoting the above narration, says: "The meaning of 'nastansikh' is that We command the angels guarding the deeds to take and record from this group those actions that warrant reward or punishment, and to disregard the rest, because the first group [of angels] writes all actions" (Al-Tibyan, Vol. 9, p. 260).

In the next verse, He describes the final stage of the Resurrection's judgment, where each group reaches the result of its actions. He says: "As for those who believed and did righteous deeds, their Lord will admit them into His mercy" (فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ فَيُدْخِلُهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ فِي رَحْمَتِهِ‌).

The use of the "fa" of consequence (fa al-tafari') here is proof that the result of keeping the account of deeds and that Divine Court of Justice is that the believers enter into Divine Mercy.

According to this verse, faith alone is not sufficient to enjoy this great blessing; rather, righteous deeds are also a condition for it.

The expression "their Lord" (rabbu-hum), which speaks of Allah's special grace, is complemented by the use of "mercy" instead of "Paradise."

And the sentence at the end of the verse, which says: "That is the manifest success" (ذلِكَ هُوَ الْفَوْزُ الْمُبِينُ‌), reaches the peak of perfection.

"Divine Mercy" is a broad concept that encompasses this world and the hereafter. In the verses of the Quran, it is applied to many meanings: sometimes to the matter of guidance, sometimes to salvation from the clutches of the enemy, sometimes to blessed rain, sometimes to other blessings such as the blessing of light and darkness, and in many cases, it is applied to Paradise and God's favors in the Resurrection.

The phrase "That is the manifest success" is repeated once more in Surah Al-An'am, verse 16. However, there, "manifest success" (al-fawz al-mubin) is used regarding those who are saved from Divine punishment: "He from whom it is averted that Day—He has granted him mercy. And that is the manifest success" (مَنْ يُصْرَفْ عَنْهُ يَوْمَئِذٍ فَقَدْ رَحِمَهُ وَ ذلِكَ الْفَوْزُ الْمُبِينُ‌). But here, it is about those who enter Paradise and Divine Mercy. In reality, both are great successes: salvation from punishment and entry into the center of Truth's mercy.

Here, a question may arise: do believers who lack righteous deeds not enter Paradise?

The answer is that they do enter, but after they face their punishment in Hell and are purified. Only those who, after the accounting, directly find their way to this center of mercy are those who, in addition to faith, also possess the capital of righteous deeds.

The word "fawz" (فوز), as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, means "victory accompanied by safety." It has been used in 19 instances in the verses of the Quran. Sometimes it is described as "manifest" (mubin) and sometimes as "great" (kabir), but in most verses, it is described as "supreme" (azim) and is usually used regarding Paradise, though it is occasionally used regarding the success of obeying the Lord, the forgiveness of sins, and the like.

In the next verse, He mentions the fate of the other group, which is the opposite of this group, saying: "But as for those who disbelieved, [it will be said]: 'Were not My verses recited to you? But you were arrogant and became a criminal people'" (وَ أَمَّا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَ فَلَمْ تَكُنْ آياتِي تُتْلى‌ عَلَيْكُمْ فَاسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ وَ كُنْتُمْ قَوْماً مُجْرِمِينَ‌).

It is noteworthy that in this verse, only disbelief (kufr) is mentioned, and evil deeds are not mentioned as a factor for entering Divine punishment. This is because the issue of disbelief alone warrants punishment, or because the expression "criminals" (mujrimin) at the end of the verse is sufficient to convey this meaning.

Another point is that here, there is no mention of the punishment of Hell, but there is mention of the Lord's rebuke and reproach, which is considered the greatest punishment, and Hell is of less significance compared to it.

It is also noteworthy that it can be inferred from this verse that without the appointment of Prophets, the sending of messengers, and the revelation of Divine verses (and, so to speak, the reinforcement of the dictates of reason by the dictates of Sharia), no punishment will be carried out by the Compassionate Lord, and this is the ultimate expression of His grace and mercy.

The final point is that the great problem of this group is, on one hand, the issue of "arrogance" (istikbar) against Divine verses, and on the other hand, the "persistence in crime and sin," which is understood from the phrase "you were a criminal people" (كُنْتُمْ قَوْماً مُجْرِمِينَ‌).

Ayahs

وَإِذَا قِيلَ إِنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ وَٱلسَّاعَةُ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهَا قُلْتُم مَّا نَدْرِى مَا ٱلسَّاعَةُ إِن نَّظُنُّ إِلَّا ظَنًّا وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمُسْتَيْقِنِينَ (٣٢)
When it was said, ‘‘Allah’s promise is indeed true, and there is no doubt about the Hour,’’ you said, ‘‘We do not know what the Hour is. We know nothing beyond conjectures, and we do not possess any certainty.’’ ’ (32)
وَبَدَا لَهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتُ مَا عَمِلُوا۟ وَحَاقَ بِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (٣٣)
The evils of what they had done will appear to them, and they will be besieged by what they used to deride. (33)
وَقِيلَ ٱلْيَوْمَ نَنسَىٰكُمْ كَمَا نَسِيتُمْ لِقَآءَ يَوْمِكُمْ هَـٰذَا وَمَأْوَىٰكُمُ ٱلنَّارُ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن نَّـٰصِرِينَ (٣٤)
And it will be said, ‘Today We will forget you, just as you forgot the encounter of this day of yours. The Fire will be your abode, and you will not have any helpers. (34)
ذَٰلِكُم بِأَنَّكُمُ ٱتَّخَذْتُمْ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ هُزُوًا وَغَرَّتْكُمُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۚ فَٱلْيَوْمَ لَا يُخْرَجُونَ مِنْهَا وَلَا هُمْ يُسْتَعْتَبُونَ (٣٥)
That is because you took the signs of Allah in derision, and the life of the world had deceived you.’ So today, they will not be brought out of it, nor will they be asked to propitiate [Allah]. (35)
فَلِلَّهِ ٱلْحَمْدُ رَبِّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَرَبِّ ٱلْأَرْضِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٣٦)
So all praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of the heavens and the Lord of the earth, the Lord of all the worlds. (36)
وَلَهُ ٱلْكِبْرِيَآءُ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٣٧)
To Him belongs all supremacy in the heavens and the earth, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (37)

Commentary

The Day When Man’s Evil Deeds Are Revealed

The first verse under discussion is, in fact, an explanation of what was briefly mentioned in the final verses of the previous section. It explains the arrogance of the disbelievers toward Divine verses and the call of the prophets. It says: "And when it was said, 'Indeed, the promise of Allah is true, and the Hour—there is no doubt about it,' you said, 'We do not know what the Hour is. We only assume an assumption, and we are not convinced'" (وَ إِذا قِيلَ إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللَّهِ حَقٌّ وَ السَّاعَةُ لا رَيْبَ فِيها قُلْتُمْ ما نَدْرِي مَا السَّاعَةُ إِنْ نَظُنُّ إِلَّا ظَنًّا وَ ما نَحْنُ بِمُسْتَيْقِنِينَ).

The expression "We do not know what the Hour is" (ما نَدْرِي مَا السَّاعَةُ), while the concept of the Resurrection was not complex for them—and if they had any doubt, it was only regarding its existence—shows that they were in a state of arrogance and indifference. Had they possessed a truth-seeking spirit, both the nature of the Day of Resurrection and the evidence for its existence would have been clear.

From here, the answer to a question raised—that if they were truly in doubt, they should bear no responsibility or sin—becomes clear. This is because their doubt did not stem from a lack of clarity in the truth, but rather from pride, vanity, stubbornness, and animosity.

It is also possible that their contradictory statements were intended as mockery and ridicule.

The next verse speaks of their punishment and retribution—a retribution that bears no resemblance to the conventional punishments of our world. It says: "And the evils of what they did will appear to them" (وَ بَدا لَهُمْ سَيِّئاتُ ما عَمِلُوا). The ugliness and evils of their deeds will be personified; they will take life and appear before them, becoming their companions and associates, constantly tormenting them! "And they will be enveloped by what they used to ridicule" (وَ حاقَ بِهِمْ ما كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ) [1].

Most painful of all is that they will be addressed by Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful: "And it will be said, 'Today We forget you just as you forgot the meeting of this Day of yours!'" (وَ قِيلَ الْيَوْمَ نَنْساكُمْ كَما نَسِيتُمْ لِقاءَ يَوْمِكُمْ هذا).

[1]: "Hāqa" (حاق) from the root "hawq" (حوق) originally means to enter, descend, strike, and encompass. Some have said its origin is "haqq" (حق) (meaning to be realized), where the first 'qaf' changed to 'waw' and then to 'alif'.

This is an expression that appears repeatedly in various forms in the Holy Quran:

In Surah Al-A'raf, verse 51, we read: "So today We forget them as they forgot the meeting of this Day of theirs" (فَالْيَوْمَ نَنْساهُمْ كَما نَسُوا لِقاءَ يَوْمِهِمْ هذا).

And in Surah As-Sajdah, verse 14, the same meaning appears in another form.

Undoubtedly, "forgetfulness" has no meaning for the Pure Essence of Allah, whose knowledge encompasses the entire universe. This is a subtle metaphor for ignoring and disregarding a criminal and sinful person. Even in our daily expressions, we say: "Forget that unfaithful friend forever," meaning treat him like a forgotten person; cease all affection, visiting, comforting, and inquiring about him, and never seek him out.

This expression is also another emphasis on the "personification of deeds" and the "proportionality of punishment to the crime," because forgetting the Resurrection causes Allah to forget them on the Day of Resurrection. How painful and soul-crushing is this great calamity—that the Merciful and Compassionate God should forget someone and deprive them of all His favors.

Commentators have mentioned various interpretations for this "forgetting," but the essence of all of them is the same comprehensive meaning mentioned above; thus, there is no need to repeat them.

Furthermore, "forgetting the meeting of the Day of Resurrection" means forgetting the meeting with all the matters and events that take place on that day, including the Reckoning and other things they used to deny. It is also possible that it refers to forgetting the meeting with Allah on that day, as the Day of Resurrection is introduced in the Holy Quran as "the Day of Meeting Allah" (Yawm Liqa' Allah), referring to inner spiritual witnessing.

The verse continues: "And it will be said to them: Your abode is the Fire!" (وَ مَأْواكُمُ النَّارُ).

And if you think someone will rush to your aid, We tell you decisively: "And there are no helpers for you" (وَ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ ناصِرِينَ).

But why and for what reason did you suffer such a fate? "That is because you took the verses of Allah in ridicule, and the worldly life deluded you" (ذلِكُمْ بِأَنَّكُمُ اتَّخَذْتُمْ آياتِ اللَّهِ هُزُواً وَ غَرَّتْكُمُ الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا). Fundamentally, these two are inseparable: "delusion" and "ridicule." Arrogant and self-important individuals who look down on others often subject them to mockery and ridicule. The primary source of pride is the goods of worldly life—power, wealth, and fleeting, temporary victories—which make "shallow" individuals so heedless that they grant the least value to the call of Divine messengers, and do not even bother to study their invitation.

At the end of the verse, it repeats and emphasizes what was in the previous verse with a different expression, saying: "So today they will not be taken out of it, nor will they be asked to appease [Allah]" (فَالْيَوْمَ لا يُخْرَجُونَ مِنْها وَ لا هُمْ يُسْتَعْتَبُونَ) [1]. There, it spoke of their fixed abode and dwelling, and here it speaks of their lack of exit from Hell. There, it said they have no helper, and here it says no excuse will be accepted from them, and consequently, there is no way of salvation for them.

At the end of this Surah, to complete the discussion on Monotheism (Tawhid) and the Hereafter (Ma'ad)—which comprised most of the Surah's topics—it describes the unity of Lordship, the greatness of Allah, and His power and wisdom within two verses, reflecting five of Allah's attributes in this section. First, it says: "Then to Allah belongs [all] praise" (فَلِلَّهِ الْحَمْدُ).

Because He is the "Lord of the heavens and Lord of the earth, Lord of the worlds" (رَبِّ السَّماواتِ وَ رَبِّ الْأَرْضِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ).

"Rabb" (Lord) means Owner, Disposer, Ruler, and Reformer. Therefore, every good and blessing comes from His Pure Essence, and for this reason, all praise returns to Him. Even the praise of a flower, the freshness of a meadow, the purity of a breeze, and the beauty of the stars is praise of Him, as all originate from His Pure Essence and are nurtured by His grace and favor.

It is interesting that He says once: "Lord of the heavens," another time "Lord of the earth," and finally "Lord of all the universe and the worlds," to shatter the belief in various lords and gods that people attributed to different beings, and to call everyone toward the Unity of Lordship (Tawhid al-Rububiyyah).

After describing His Pure Essence with the stations of "Praise" and "Lordship," in the third description He adds: "And to Him belongs [all] majesty in the heavens and the earth" (وَ لَهُ الْكِبْرِياءُ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ).

For the signs of His greatness are manifest across the expanse of the heavens, throughout the earth, and the entire universe.

In the previous verse, the talk was of the station of Lordship—meaning His ownership and management of the universe—and here the talk is of His greatness, which we become more acquainted with the more we contemplate the creation of the heavens and the earth.

Finally, in the fourth and fifth descriptions, He says: "And He is the All-Mighty, the All-Wise" (وَ هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ).

In this way, the collection of "Knowledge, Power, Greatness, Lordship, and Praiseworthiness"—which is a set of His most important attributes and Beautiful Names—is completed.

It is as if it points to this: "To Him belongs praise, so praise Him; He is the Lord, so thank Him; to Him belongs majesty, so magnify Him; and He is the All-Mighty, the All-Wise, so obey Him."

Thus, Surah Al-Jathiyah, which began with the description of Allah as "the All-Mighty, the All-Wise," ends with these same attributes, and its entire content bears witness to His infinite Might and Wisdom.

O Lord! By Your majesty and greatness, and by Your station of Lordship, Might, and Wisdom, keep us steadfast on the path of obeying Your command. O Allah! All praise and thanks belong to You, and every success we achieve is from the blessing of Your boundless favors; sustain these blessings for us and increase them.

O God! We are all drowned in Your benevolence; grant us the success to offer thanks to You. Amin, O Lord of the Worlds.

End of Surah Al-Jathiyah 14 Sha'ban 1405 (May 15, 1985)
